summary:
The Kama Sutra (composed c. 2nd–4th centuries AD) by Vātsyāyana is one of the most famous and most misunderstood texts of the ancient world — a comprehensive Sanskrit treatise on the nature of love, relationships, household management, and human flourishing within the framework of the classical Indian concept of the four aims of life (dharma, artha, kama, moksha). While infamous in the Western popular imagination purely for its sexual content, the text is actually a sophisticated social manual covering a wide range of topics: how to set up a comfortable home, how to court a prospective partner, the dynamics of marriage, the psychology of desire, and the social codes governing amorous life in Gupta-era Indian cities.

Vātsyāyana drew on earlier texts in the kāmaśāstra (science of love) tradition and compiled them into a systematic treatise divided into seven books. The sexual positions for which the work is famous in the West occupy only a portion of one book; the remainder deals with courtship rituals, types of relationships, social conduct, and a fascinating window into the sophisticated urban aristocratic culture of classical India. The text assumes a wealthy, educated, urban male reader navigating a complex social world. The Kama Sutra was translated into English by Richard Burton and F.F. Arbuthnot in 1883 — a culturally charged translation that helped spark Victorian-era debates about sexuality and served as an early text in comparative sexology. It remains one of the few ancient Sanskrit texts widely known in the modern West.
